annotations.label: Gauss's law

anchor.snippet: Gauss's law describes the relationship between an electric field and electric charges

annotations.note: The physical law ∇·E = ρ/ε₀ is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss's law for magnetism

anchor.snippet: Gauss's law for magnetism states that electric charges have no magnetic analogues

annotations.note: The physical law ∇·B = 0 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Faraday's law of induction

anchor.snippet: describes how a time-varying magnetic field corresponds to the negative curl of an electric field

annotations.note: Faraday's law is not formalized; Mathlib lacks a curl operator entirely.

annotations.label: Rotating bar magnet generator

anchor.snippet: a rotating bar magnet creates a changing magnetic field and generates an electric field in a nearby wire

annotations.note: A physical example with no mathematical formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ampère–Maxwell law

anchor.snippet: The original law of Ampère states that magnetic fields relate to electric current

annotations.note: The Ampère–Maxwell law is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of self-sustaining EM waves

anchor.snippet: the existence of self-sustaining electromagnetic waves which travel through empty space

annotations.note: No electromagnetic wave existence result is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz force law

anchor.section: Microscopic formulation in terms of electric and magnetic fields (in vacuum version)
anchor.snippet: the Lorentz force law, describes how the electric and magnetic fields act on charged particles and currents

annotations.note: The Lorentz force F = q(E + v×B) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Electric and magnetic fields

anchor.snippet: The equations introduce the electric field

annotations.note: The physical E and B fields are not defined in Mathlib (general vector fields exist but not these).

annotations.label: Total electric charge density

anchor.snippet: the total electric charge density (total charge per unit volume)

annotations.note: Charge density as a physical quantity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Total electric current density

anchor.snippet: the total electric current density (total current per unit area)

annotations.note: Current density as a physical quantity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universal constants ε₀, μ₀, c

anchor.snippet: The universal constants appearing in the equations

annotations.note: Physical constants ε₀, μ₀, c are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient operator (del)

anchor.snippet: denotes the three-dimensional gradient operator, del

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `gradient` (notation ∇) via the Riesz representation of the derivative on any inner-product space, generalizing the 3D del.

annotations.label: Divergence operator

anchor.snippet: denotes the divergence operator

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone divergence operator; divergence as a sum of partial derivatives appears only implicitly inside the divergence theorem.

annotations.label: Curl operator

anchor.snippet: denotes the curl operator

annotations.note: Mathlib has no curl operator on vector fields.

annotations.label: Volume with closed boundary surface

anchor.snippet: Ω is any volume with closed boundary surface ∂Ω

annotations.note: Mathlib's divergence theorem uses boxes, not general 3D volumes with arbitrary closed boundary surfaces.

annotations.label: Surface with closed boundary curve

anchor.snippet: Σ is any surface with closed boundary curve ∂Σ

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a notion of an embedded surface with closed boundary curve for Stokes' theorem.

annotations.label: Differentiation under the integral sign

anchor.snippet: we can bring the differentiation under the integral sign in Faraday's law

annotations.note: Mathlib's parametric-integral lemmas give the Leibniz rule (derivative of an integral equals integral of the derivative) under domination, more general than needed here.

anchors.snippet: we can bring the differentiation under the integral sign in Faraday's law

annotations.label: Total enclosed electric charge

anchor.snippet: The total electric charge Q enclosed in Ω is the volume integral over Ω of the charge density ρ

annotations.note: This is a physical volume integral of charge density, not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The total electric charge Q enclosed in Ω is the volume integral over Ω of the charge density ρ

annotations.label: Net magnetic flux

anchor.snippet: The net magnetic flux Φ B is the surface integral of the magnetic field B passing through a fixed surface

annotations.note: Surface flux of a vector field through a surface is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The net magnetic flux Φ B is the surface integral of the magnetic field B passing through a fixed surface

annotations.label: Net electric flux

anchor.snippet: The net electric flux Φ E is the surface integral of the electric field E passing through Σ

annotations.note: Surface flux of a vector field is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The net electric flux Φ E is the surface integral of the electric field E passing through Σ

annotations.label: Net electric current

anchor.snippet: The net electric current I is the surface integral of the electric current density J passing through Σ

annotations.note: Surface integral of current density is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The net electric current I is the surface integral of the electric current density J passing through Σ

annotations.note: The Gaussian-unit form of Maxwell's equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalence of differential and integral forms

anchor.section: Relationship between differential and integral formulations
anchor.snippet: The equivalence of the differential and integral formulations are a consequence of the Gauss divergence theorem and the Kelvin–Stokes theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has the box divergence theorem but not the full Kelvin–Stokes theorem, so the Maxwell differential/integral equivalence is only partially supported.

annotations.label: Divergence theorem for electric flux

anchor.snippet: the electric flux through the boundary surface ∂Ω can be rewritten as

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the divergence theorem only for rectangular boxes, a special case of the general Gauss theorem over an arbitrary volume.

annotations.label: Integral and differential Gauss's law equivalence

anchor.snippet: this is satisfied if and only if the integrand is zero everywhere

annotations.note: Mathlib's fundamental lemma of the calculus of variations gives that a function whose integrals against all test functions vanish is a.e. zero, the underlying 'integrand vanishes' principle.

anchors.snippet: this is satisfied if and only if the integrand is zero everywhere

annotations.note: The conclusion ∇·B = 0 is a physical statement not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kelvin–Stokes theorem for circulation

anchor.snippet: By the Kelvin–Stokes theorem we can rewrite the line integrals of the fields around the closed boundary curve ∂Σ

annotations.note: Mathlib proves only the planar (2D Green's theorem) special case, not the full Kelvin–Stokes theorem for surfaces in 3D.

anchors.snippet: By the Kelvin–Stokes theorem we can rewrite the line integrals of the fields around the closed boundary curve ∂Σ

annotations.label: Fluid dynamics circulation analogy

anchor.snippet: the circulation of a fluid is the line integral of the fluid's flow velocity field around a closed loop

annotations.note: A physical analogy with no specific formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Charge conservation (continuity equation)

anchor.snippet: The invariance of charge can be derived as a corollary of Maxwell's equations

annotations.note: The continuity equation ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·J = 0 is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The invariance of charge can be derived as a corollary of Maxwell's equations

annotations.note: Magnetization M is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Homogeneous vs inhomogeneous materials

anchor.snippet: For homogeneous materials, ε and μ are constant throughout the material

annotations.note: The material classification is a physics concept not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isotropic vs anisotropic materials

anchor.snippet: For isotropic materials, ε and μ are scalars

annotations.note: The isotropy classification of materials is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dispersive materials

anchor.snippet: Materials are generally dispersive , so ε and μ depend on the frequency of any incident EM waves

annotations.note: Dispersion (frequency-dependent ε, μ) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-linear materials

anchor.snippet: D and P are not necessarily proportional to E

annotations.note: Nonlinear constitutive behavior is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relativistic invariance of Maxwell's equations

anchor.snippet: the Maxwell equations are relativistically invariant

annotations.note: Lorentz invariance of Maxwell's equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor calculus formulation

anchor.snippet: the electromagnetic tensor F αβ is an antisymmetric covariant order 2 tensor

annotations.note: The electromagnetic field tensor F_αβ is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Differential form formulation

anchor.snippet: is the electromagnetic tensor considered as a 2-form

annotations.note: The electromagnetic 2-form and dF = 0, d⋆F = J are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conformal invariance of field equations

anchor.snippet: the differential form field equations are conformally invariant

annotations.note: Conformal invariance of the field equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniqueness requires boundary and initial conditions

anchor.snippet: are necessary for a unique solution

annotations.note: Uniqueness of solutions to Maxwell's equations under boundary/initial data is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jefimenko's equations (explicit solution)

anchor.snippet: are the explicit solution to Maxwell's equations for the electric and magnetic fields created by any given distribution of charges and currents

annotations.note: Jefimenko's retarded-potential solutions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Redundancy of the two Gauss's laws

anchor.snippet: any system satisfying Faraday's law and Ampère's circuital law automatically also satisfies the two Gauss's laws

annotations.note: This consistency/redundancy result for Maxwell's equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No magnetic charge (monopoles)

anchor.snippet: Maxwell's equations posit that there is electric charge , but no magnetic charge

annotations.note: The absence of magnetic monopoles is a physical postulate not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetry under E–B interchange

anchor.snippet: the resulting four equations would be fully symmetric under the interchange of electric and magnetic fields

annotations.note: The electromagnetic duality symmetry is not formalized in Mathlib.
